headline: Children's voices will now shape how AI is built
meta_description: A new international coalition launched in Geneva is making children's rights central to AI development, not an afterthought.
summary_short: A groundbreaking coalition brings together 17 countries and UN agencies to ensure children's perspectives shape AI from the start.
body:
Children live increasingly inside worlds shaped by artificial intelligence—from the learning apps on their screens to the algorithms deciding what they see. Until now, their protection has mostly come after the fact. A new coalition launching this week in Geneva is flipping that approach entirely.

The Coalition for Children's Rights and Protection in the Age of Artificial Intelligence, unveiled at the UN Global Dialogue on AI Governance, brings together governments, tech companies, educators and child welfare experts around a single foundation: the Convention on the Rights of the Child, the world's most widely ratified human rights treaty.

Seventeen countries have already signed on—from Austria and Canada to Kenya, Japan and Spain—alongside UN agencies including UNICEF and UNESCO. Their commitment is radical in its simplicity: children should not be treated merely as users needing protection, but as rights holders whose voices must genuinely shape how AI systems are designed in the first place.

It's a meaningful departure from consultation exercises that arrive too late. Coalition members are pledging to weave children's perspectives into every stage—design, rollout, oversight—making it a legal obligation rather than an optional courtesy. The shift recognizes what young people already know: AI will reshape their education, creativity and connections. They deserve a say in how.
